CPCtelera examples | Ejemplos incluidos en CPCtelera que muestran gráficos, sonido, entradas y bucles de juego. | Referencia preferente de ejemplos listos para compilar y ejecutar.

CPCWiki Programming Board | Foro general de programación en CPC con hilos sobre gráficos, sonido, trucos de memoria y engines. | Tratar como fuente de contexto y ejemplos aislados, no como documentación formal.

CPCtelera on macOS (engine + toolchain) | CPCtelera game engine and cross-development toolchain with official support for OS X/macOS. | When user is on macOS, propose installing CPCtelera by cloning the Git repo and running ./setup.sh as per official docs.

Aseprite for macOS | Commercial pixel art and sprite editor with official macOS builds, ideal for CPC-style graphics. | Mention Aseprite when user wants a modern, user-friendly sprite editor and is fine with paying for a license.

GrafX2 for macOS | Open-source 256-color pixel-art editor available for macOS via native builds or package managers. | Propose GrafX2 as an open-source option, especially when user prefers free tools or 256-color workflows.

Homebrew on macOS | The de facto standard package manager on macOS, used to install CLI tools and cask apps like Retro Virtual Machine. | When user is on macOS and lacks dependencies, suggest installing Homebrew first and then using it for the rest of the stack.

Keyboard Scanning Directly Using Hardware on Amstrad CPC
Oct 23, 2020

This document describes how the keyboard matrix is accessed on Amstrad CPC 464/6128 without the firmware. The two chips responsible for this are the Intel 8255 (PPI), and AY-3-8912 (PSG).

Intel 8255 (PPI), AY-3-8912 (PSG) and Keyboard Matrix Fig. 1 (source: Cpcwiki)

How Keyboard Matrix Bits Are Sampled

Keyboard Matrix

Example

1 Intel 8255 (PPI)
8255 allows sharing the CPU bus with multiple I/O components. It has three 8-bit port registers, and a control register for setting the direction of these ports. For keyboard purposes, we are interested in Port A, Port C, and the control register.

Port A is connected to AY-3-8912's data pins. Port C's upper two bits are connected

A is connected to AY-3-8912's data pins. Port C's upper two bits are connected to AY-3-8912's control registers BDIR and BC1. These are outlined with a blue rectangle in Figure 1. Port C's lower 4 bits are connected to 74ls145 decoder, which then is connected to the keyboard matrix rows. Of the 4-bits, only from 0 to 10 are used for selecting the bit line. Keyboard columns are handled by the AY-3-8912. These are outlined with a green rectangle in Figure 1.

2 AY-3-8912 (PSG)
AY-3-8912 is a soun

lined with a green rectangle in Figure 1.

2 AY-3-8912 (PSG)
AY-3-8912 is a sound generator, but also contains two general purpose I/O port registers. One of the ports is connected to keyboard matrix columns. With the PPI's 10 bit lines, this allows sampling 10*8, 80 unique keys from the keyboard.

3 How Keyboard Matrix Bits Are Sampled
The main idea is to configure PPI and PSG so that we select the bit line we want to sample. This involves the following steps:

Set PPI's Port A and C as output:

Set PPI's Port A and C as output:

text
ld     bc, #f782
out    (c), c
Select PSG's register 14 on Port A:

text
ld     bc, #f40e
out    (c), c
Latch PSG register:

text
ld     bc, #f6c0
out    (c), c
ld     bc, #f600
out    (c), c
Set PPI's Port A as input and C as output:

text
ld     bc, #f792
out    (c), c
Select the keyboard matrix row want to sample:
text
ld     bc, #f640
out    (c), c
Sample the value on Port A:

text
ld     b, #f4
in     a,
